title: How reentrancy actually works
description: An external call can hand control to attacker code that calls back into the original function before its state finished updating.
body: Use when reasoning about why external calls are dangerous. When a contract sends ETH or calls another contract, the recipient's code runs synchronously and can call back into the original function; if the original updates state after the call, the re-entrant call sees stale state (e.g. an un-decremented balance) and can withdraw repeatedly. This is not concurrency but synchronous nested execution. Do not perform external calls before finalizing state. Actions: apply checks-effects-interactions and a reentrancy guard, and treat every value transfer as a potential callback. Validation signals include an adversarial reentrancy test that fails to drain funds. Risk boundary: reentrancy has caused some of the largest losses in the ecosystem; treat any external call preceding a state update as a critical exposure.
